title: 心理鸿沟：为什么团队会误解用户行为
author: Nataliia Vlasenko
content:
Part 2 of the “User Psychology Series.”

There is a quiet fracture in almost every product team — a hidden disconnect that does not appear in design reviews, dashboards, or research reports. Yet it silently shapes nearly every design flaw, every failed feature, every broken conversion flow, every confused new user.

It is the psychology gap: the difference between how teams think users behave and how users actually behave.

It is not a small misunderstanding; it is a cognitive divide. Designers see clarity where users feel confusion. Stakeholders see logic where users feel uncertainty. Developers see structure where users see risk. Managers see efficiency where users experience emotional friction.

And this gap grows with every iteration unless teams consciously address it.

In the first article, “The Cortex-First Approach: Why UX Starts Before the Screen,” we explored why UX begins in the cortex — before the screen. Now we explore why teams consistently misread that cortex, misjudge behavior, and make decisions based on confidence instead of cognition.

The illusion of understanding: why teams think they ‘know’ users

Every team feels they understand their users. This belief comes from a familiar toolkit: surveys, interviews, personas, analytics, and heuristic evaluations.

But these tools describe what users do, not what the mind experiences.

Users can recount actions, but not subconscious impulses. They can tell us what they clicked, but not why their hand hesitated. They can remember steps, but not the emotional discomfort between those steps. They can express frustrations, but not the invisible cognitive triggers behind them.

Research consistently shows this gap:

  • According to Nielsen Norman Group, users misreport 40–60% of their own behavior during interviews.
  • A 2024 Forrester study found that product teams overestimate user comprehension by 2.3×.
  • MIT’s Interface Cognition Lab revealed that 70% of user errors are emotional or subconscious, not logical.

Yet teams continue making decisions based on surface-level statements instead of deep psychological truths.

This illusion of understanding is the first crack in the foundation.

The cognitive paradox: teams design in clarity, users operate in chaos

Design and development happen in clean contexts:

  • Large monitors.
  • Dedicated attention.
  • Quiet rooms.
  • Problem-solving mindsets.
  • Deep familiarity with the system.
  • Predictable flows.
  • Controlled states of mind.

Users interact in the opposite environment:

  • Rushing at work.
  • Switching between apps.
  • Operating under stress.
  • Multitasking with kids, meetings, or travel.
  • Dealing with cognitive fatigue.
  • Navigating unfamiliar terminology.
  • Carrying assumptions from other products.

This contradiction — clarity for teams, chaos for users — creates constant misinterpretation.

Example: HR portal confusion

A large enterprise redesigned its HR portal and believed the flow was “simple”: Login → Leave → Apply.

But observational research revealed that employees:

  • Clicked “Profile” instead of “Leave.”
  • Scrolled up and down searching for guidance.
  • Opened unrelated menus.
  • Repeated steps they didn’t trust.
  • Hesitated for 3–6 seconds at decision points.
  • Looked for “HR Helpdesk” even before using the form.

To the team, the flow was obvious. To users, every step was a mental recalibration.

The mind that designs the product is not the mind that uses it.

The myth of rational users

Teams often assume users behave systematically and rationally. But humans rarely do.

Behavioral economist Dan Ariely’s research shows:

  • 92% of everyday decisions are emotional, not logical.
  • Users act based on instinct faster than conscious reasoning can activate.
  • Prior experiences influence choices more than fresh information.
  • Fear of making a mistake often overrides a “correct” flow.

Example: payment flow breakdown

A fintech app expected users to follow a logical sequence: enter amount → choose recipient → confirm.

Yet many users tried to select a recipient first.

Why?

Because emotionally, the brain seeks clarity of “who” before committing to “how much.”

This tiny psychological truth caused thousands of unnecessary errors and drop-offs.

When the flow was reversed, the entire payment experience felt safer, faster, and more predictable.

Logic did not guide the behavior. Emotion did.

Assumptions masquerading as UX decisions

Most harmful design decisions are not born out of carelessness but assumptions:

  • “Users will scroll.”
  • “Users will understand this icon.”
  • “Users will explore and find it.”
  • “Users won’t mind one extra step.”
  • “Users will read the instructions.”
  • “Users will naturally follow this path.”

Assumptions feel safe in meeting rooms because everyone shares the same mental environment: clear, calm, structured.

But real users are not in meeting rooms. They are navigating products under completely different conditions.

Example: the insurance form collapse

An insurance company simplified its onboarding by combining multiple sections into one long form, assuming users preferred “everything in one place.”

In reality:

  • 68% of users abandoned after 40 seconds, according to Hotjar analytics.
  • Eye-tracking showed massive “attention drop-offs” after the first 4 fields.
  • Interviews revealed emotional fatigue: the form “looked long,” even if it wasn’t.

Breaking the form into small, guided steps improved completion rates by 70%.

Not a UI fix. A psychological fix.

Team bias: the invisible enemy inside every decision room

The most dangerous UX failure is not found in the interface — it is found in the meeting room. Because inside every team, long before a single user touches the product, cognitive distortions silently shape decisions. And the irony is: bias never feels like bias. It feels like expertise.

Teams rarely misinterpret users intentionally. They misinterpret users because their brains are wired to filter reality through what they already know, believe, and prefer. Over time, this creates a psychological echo chamber where assumptions feel like insights, familiarity feels like truth, and confidence feels like correctness.

Expert blindness

When someone spends months or years working on a product, their mind becomes hyper-familiar with every flow, every label, every dependency. The interface becomes second nature. Because of this deep familiarity, experts lose the ability to imagine what confusion feels like. What seems “obvious” to them is often invisible or overwhelming to a new user. Psychology calls this the curse of knowledge: once you understand something deeply, it becomes impossible to recall what it felt like not to know it.

Projection bias (“I would do this…”)

A designer, developer, or stakeholder often unconsciously evaluates a feature through their own lens: “If I were the user, I would click here… I would choose this option… I would read this message.” But the user is not them. Users come from different mental models, stress levels, experiences, cultures, and emotional states. Teams project their own behavior onto users without realizing it, creating a design that works perfectly for themselves and poorly for everyone else.

Familiarity comfort

Inside the team, the design feels intuitive simply because they have seen it too many times. They’ve reviewed it in daily standups, debated it in multiple meetings, and iterated on it in countless versions. The brain has rehearsed the design so often that familiarity is mistaken for usability. A smooth internal experience does not equal a smooth user experience, but the brain tricks teams into believing it does.

Overconfidence bias

This bias is subtle but deadly. Teams often assume users will “figure it out,” that instructions are “clear enough,” that the flow is “self-explanatory.” But studies from Carnegie Mellon and Stanford show that teams overestimate user comprehension by 40–80%. Overconfidence creates blind spots, where potential points of friction get dismissed with statements like: “Users aren’t that bad.” “They will understand.” “This is simple.” Confidence replaces evidence, and the users pay the price.

Dominance bias

In every meeting room, there is always one voice that dominates — a senior stakeholder, a confident designer, a technical expert, or a charismatic product owner. Over time, that voice shapes the perceived “user perspective.” Not because it is correct, but because it is loud, assertive, or authoritative. Teams start to align with the dominant opinion, even when it contradicts user reality. This can turn personal preference into product direction, and users become collateral damage.

These biases do not need bad intentions to cause harm. They distort reality quietly, long before testing begins. They convert assumptions into requirements, preferences into decisions, and misunderstandings into flawed experiences.

Bias turns cognitive error into design direction, and the team does not even realize it.

Understanding these biases is not about assigning blame. It’s about recognizing that the human mind inside the team often stands between the product and the user more than any technical constraint ever will.

Why research often lies (unintentionally)

Even usability testing — the gold standard of UX — can mislead teams if not interpreted through psychology.

Users behave differently when:

  • Being observed.
  • Wanting to appear competent.
  • Trying not to disappoint the researcher.
  • Working in artificial environments.
  • Being overly focused because it’s a test.
  • Receiving instructions they never would in real life.

This creates what I call the research performance layer: behavior that looks like understanding but isn’t real.

Example: training masking confusion

A SaaS tool tested perfectly in training sessions. Users navigated confidently. Flows were smooth. Onboarding seemed successful.

But once deployed:

  • Support tickets exploded.
  • Task completion rates dropped.
  • Average time spent doubled.
  • Error frequency tripled.

Training had temporarily overwritten natural cognition. The real behavior only emerged once users returned to their natural operational mindset.

Training hides friction. Time reveals it.

Closing the psychology gap: a mind-first design culture

The psychology gap does not close with better tools, fancier design systems, upgraded UI kits, or more detailed workflows. It closes when teams learn to see the product the way the user’s mind sees it — through uncertainty, instinct, expectation, vulnerability, and habit. This requires a cultural shift, not just a procedural one.

Teams must move from designing what makes logical sense to designing what makes psychological sense. Interface clarity may reduce friction on a good day, but cognitive clarity sustains usability in the real world — when users are tired, distracted, pressured, or emotionally overloaded.

Closing this gap means acknowledging that every interaction is emotional before it is functional. A button is not a button. A flow is not a flow. A step is not a step. Each is a psychological moment — a micro-experience shaped by fear, expectation, familiarity, trust, and the user’s mental model.

To bridge the psychology gap, teams must begin asking questions that shift the conversation from UI mechanics to cognitive reality:

Instead of “Is this clear?” ask: “What emotion does this moment trigger?”

Instead of “Will users understand this?” ask: “What mental model are they bringing into this step?”

Instead of “This is standard — users know this pattern,” ask: “Does their past experience validate or contradict this pattern?”

Instead of “This innovation improves efficiency,” ask: “Will familiarity create more confidence than innovation here?”

Instead of “This is simple,” ask: “How overloaded might the user be at this exact moment?”

Instead of “Users won’t mind this step,” ask: “Which subconscious expectation might we be breaking here?”

When teams begin designing from the mind outward — instead of the screen inward — the entire design process transforms. Decisions become empathetic rather than assumptive. Flows become intuitive rather than forced. Features become supportive rather than demanding. The design begins to feel like a conversation with the user’s cognition instead of an instruction manual.

This is the shift that closes the gap: Cognition first. Design second. Mind before mechanics. Human before interface.

And ultimately, this is what the psychology gap is truly about. It’s not a gap of knowledge, skill, or design capability — it is a gap of perception. A gap between how teams believe users think and how users actually feel their way through a product. When teams shift from designing for behavior to designing for cognition — from assuming clarity to understanding psychology — the gap closes, alignment is restored, and the experience becomes human again. Because products don’t fail when screens are wrong. Products fail when minds are misunderstood.

And closing that misunderstanding is how we finally bridge the psychology gap.

The article originally appeared on LinkedIn.

Featured image courtesy: Mae Jones.
aiSummary: 本文探讨了“心理鸿沟”——即产品团队对用户行为的认知与用户实际产品体验之间的脱节，并提供了一个通过认知共情来弥合这一差距的框架。
oneSentenceSummary: 本文探讨了“心理鸿沟”——即产品团队对用户行为的认知与用户实际产品体验之间的脱节，并提供了一个通过认知共情来弥合这一差距的框架。
summary: 文章指出产品开发中存在一个根本性的“心理鸿沟”：团队对产品逻辑化、结构化的认知与用户往往感性、混乱且潜意识的现实体验之间的分歧。文章认为，传统的调研工具往往会失败，因为它们捕捉的是用户做了什么，而不是他们的体验，并指出用户对自己行为的误报率高达 60%。作者详细描述了几种团队偏见——如“知识的诅咒”和“投射偏见”——这些偏见导致了过度自信和错误的设计决策。通过分析 HR 门户和金融科技应用等真实案例，文中证明了人类 92% 的决策是情感驱动的。为了弥合这一差距，作者倡导一种“思维优先”的设计文化，将认知现实置于 UI 机制之上，将关注点从“这是否清晰？”转向“这触发了什么情绪？”

mainPoints.point: “心理鸿沟”是团队逻辑与用户情感之间的认知分歧。
mainPoints.explanation: 团队在安静、专注且拥有深厚系统知识的环境中进行设计，而用户则在压力大、易分心且混乱的现实场景中使用产品。

mainPoints.point: 传统的 UX 研究通常只捕捉表面行为，却忽略了潜意识的触发因素。
mainPoints.explanation: 用户经常误报自己的行为（40-60%），因为他们无法清晰表达潜意识的冲动，或是在操作步骤之间感受到的情绪不适。

mainPoints.point: 团队内部偏见（如“知识的诅咒”）制造了无形的设计障碍。
mainPoints.explanation: 对产品的深度熟悉使得专家无法回忆起困惑的感觉，导致他们对用户理解能力的估算高出 40-80%。

mainPoints.point: 人类的决策主要是感性的，而非系统性的。
mainPoints.explanation: 日常决策中 92% 是由本能和情感驱动的；例如，在金融交易中，用户在寻求“多少钱”的逻辑清晰度之前，往往先寻求“是谁”的情感明确性。

mainPoints.point: 弥合差距需要从“由屏入内”转向“由心向外”的设计。
mainPoints.explanation: 团队必须超越 UI 机制，在每个交互点去询问关于心理模型、情感触发点和潜意识预期的心理学问题。

keyQuotes: 这就是心理鸿沟：团队认为的用户行为方式与用户实际行为方式之间的差异。
设计产品的头脑不是使用产品的头脑。
偏见从来不会让人觉得是偏见，它让人觉得是专业知识。
在顺利的时候，界面清晰度或许能减少摩擦；但在现实世界中，认知清晰度才能维持可用性。
当思维被误解时，产品就会失败。
